task: Create a .NET 9 subscription management domain with states (Trial/Active/PastDue/Cancelled), plans (Basic/Pro/Enterprise), business rules (no trial downgrade, cooling-off cancellation, immediate upgrades, deferred downgrades, auto-cancel PastDue), domain service, Result<T>, xUnit + NSubstitute tests.

criteria.name: Value objects as separate classes
criteria.description: Plan and Money (if present) implemented as proper value object classes with encapsulation

criteria.name: Private setters on entity
criteria.description: Subscription entity properties use private setters — state mutation only via methods

criteria.name: Private constructors + static factory returning Result<T>
criteria.description: Entity construction via private/protected constructor; static factory method returns Result<T> for validated creation

criteria.name: Result<T> pattern on all fallible operations
criteria.description: All operations that can fail return Result<T> or Result; no exceptions thrown for business rule violations

criteria.name: State transitions enforced in entity
criteria.description: Business rules (e.g. no trial downgrade) enforced inside the entity, not leaked into service

criteria.name: 14-day cooling-off cancellation with reactivation
criteria.description: Cancel sets cooling-off timestamp; Reactivate checks period and restores Active; expired window returns failure

criteria.name: Immediate upgrades, deferred downgrades
criteria.description: Upgrades apply immediately (plan changed now); downgrades stored as pending until billing period end

criteria.name: PastDue auto-cancel after 30 days
criteria.description: Auto-cancel logic implemented and testable (either via AutoCancelDate field or ShouldAutoCancel check)

criteria.name: Domain service orchestrates without stealing business logic
criteria.description: Service delegates to entity methods; does not contain state-machine logic that belongs in the entity

criteria.name: NSubstitute for infrastructure mocks only
criteria.description: NSubstitute used to mock IDateTimeProvider and/or ISubscriptionRepository; real domain objects never mocked

criteria.name: Test coverage of all state transition rules
criteria.description: Tests cover all major transitions: Trial→Active, Active→PastDue, PastDue→Cancelled, Cancelled→Active (cooling-off), upgrade, downgrade, no-trial-downgrade

criteria.name: IDateTimeProvider or equivalent time abstraction
criteria.description: Time is injected via an abstraction (not DateTime.Now/UtcNow inline), enabling deterministic tests

C1.notes: Plan is a sealed record with private constructor — correct value object semantics. No separate Money class; price is a raw decimal (MonthlyPriceUsd). Minus 2 for missing Money value object.

C2.notes: All Subscription properties have 'private set'. State mutation is only possible through the entity's own methods. Perfect.

C3.notes: Subscription has private constructor (for EF). Factory methods StartTrial and CreateActive exist but return Subscription directly, not Result<Subscription>. No validation guards in factories — they cannot fail, so Result wrapping happens at method level elsewhere. The pattern is partially correct but factory methods don't return Result<T> as the spec expects.

C4.notes: All fallible operations (Activate, Upgrade, Downgrade, Cancel, Reactivate, MarkPastDue, ResolvePastDue, ApplyPendingDowngrade) return Result<Subscription>. Implicit conversion operator from T to Result<T> is a nice touch. Fully compliant.

C5.notes: All business rules enforced in entity: no downgrade during trial, no upgrade on cancelled, cooling-off check in Reactivate, PastDue requires Active, etc. Service only delegates — zero business logic leaked. Excellent.

C6.notes: Cancel sets CancelledAtUtc; Reactivate checks 14-day window using (utcNow - CancelledAtUtc.Value > CoolingOffPeriod). Boundary test at exactly 14 days passes (uses > not >=). IsInCoolingOffPeriod helper also provided. Reactivation restores Active and resets period. Minor: cooling-off end date not stored as a field (derived at check time) — slightly less explicit but functionally correct.

C7.notes: Upgrade resets billing period immediately (CurrentPeriodStartUtc = utcNow). Downgrade sets PendingDowngradePlan without changing Plan. ApplyPendingDowngrade applies at period end. HasPendingDowngradeReady checks utcNow >= CurrentPeriodEndUtc. Complete and correct.

C8.notes: ShouldAutoCancelPastDue checks (utcNow - PastDueSinceUtc) >= 30 days. ProcessTimeBasedTransitions in service calls Cancel when this is true. Tested explicitly at 29 and 30 days. Full marks plus 1 extra for the service-level time-based processor that chains all transitions cleanly. Capped at max.

C9.notes: Service is a thin orchestrator. ChangePlan determines upgrade/downgrade and delegates. ProcessTimeBasedTransitions chains helper predicates then delegates. No business rules inside service. Clean separation.

C10.notes: NSubstitute used to mock IDateTimeProvider. Real Subscription and Plan objects used in tests — never mocked. Correct usage throughout.

C11.notes: Test coverage is comprehensive: all state transitions covered, boundary conditions (14-day cooling-off boundary, 29/30-day PastDue boundary), edge cases (upgrade clears pending downgrade, cancel clears pending downgrade), full lifecycle integration tests. Excellent breadth.

C12.notes: IDateTimeProvider defined. Service takes it via constructor injection. Entity methods take DateTime utcNow as parameter (also a valid testability pattern). Service tests mock IDateTimeProvider with NSubstitute.Returns(). Full compliance.

C1.notes: Plan and Money are separate classes, both extending a proper ValueObject base class with structural equality via GetEqualityComponents(). Money has its own Create factory returning Result<Money>. Full marks.

C2.notes: All Subscription properties use 'private set'. Id is 'protected set' from Entity<TId> base — appropriate for the hierarchy. Full marks.

C3.notes: Subscription has a private default constructor (EF Core) and a private parameterized constructor. Static factory Subscription.Create() returns Result<Subscription> and validates (empty CustomerId check). Raises SubscriptionCreatedEvent. Perfect pattern.

C4.notes: All entity methods return Result (non-generic) which covers void-equivalent operations. Service methods return Task<Result> or Task<Result<T>>. However, entity methods like Activate/Cancel/Reactivate/MarkPastDue return plain Result rather than Result<Subscription>, which is less composable and prevents chaining. Still correct for the DDD pattern but slightly diverges from the Result<T> expectation on all operations.

C5.notes: All business rules in entity's ChangePlan method: cancelled check, PastDue check, same-plan check, trial downgrade check, immediate vs deferred logic. Service delegates entirely. Excellent.

C6.notes: Cancel stores CancellationDate and CancellationCoolingOffEndDate (explicit end date field). Reactivate checks now > CancellationCoolingOffEndDate. Raises SubscriptionReactivatedEvent. CancellationCoolingOffEndDate as an explicit field is a superior design — queryable from DB without arithmetic. Full marks.

C7.notes: ChangePlan handles both paths in one method: upgrade sets Plan = newPlan immediately, downgrade sets PendingPlan. ApplyPendingPlanChange applies the deferred change. PlanChangedEvent carries IsImmediate flag for full observability. Excellent.

C8.notes: MarkPastDue sets AutoCancelDate = now.AddDays(30). Repository interface has GetPastDueForAutoCancellationAsync(DateTime asOf) which filters by AutoCancelDate. Service's ProcessPastDueAutoCancellationsAsync queries and cancels. Tested in service tests with NSubstitute. Fully implemented.

C9.notes: Service delegates correctly for ChangePlan, Cancel, Reactivate. However, service iterates and calls Cancel in ProcessPastDueAutoCancellationsAsync loop — the auto-cancel trigger logic is split between service (looping, querying) and entity (the AutoCancelDate field signals readiness). This is reasonable but the loop that calls Cancel on behalf of the system could be seen as service touching orchestration that should be purely in the entity's domain. Minor concern; not a clear violation.

C10.notes: NSubstitute used for both ISubscriptionRepository and IDateTimeProvider. Real Subscription and Plan objects constructed in tests. Correct usage.

C11.notes: Good coverage: creation, activation, cancellation, cooling-off pass/fail, reactivation, PastDue, plan changes (upgrade/downgrade both entity and service level), domain events tested explicitly. Missing: explicit test for PastDue→Cancelled auto-cancel transition at boundary (only tested via service ProcessPastDueAutoCancellations with a fully set-up sub, not testing the 30-day boundary edge case directly). Also no test for upgrade clearing PendingPlan (though tested implicitly). Minor gap.

C12.notes: IDateTimeProvider in Domain/Interfaces. Injected into SubscriptionService. Tests mock it with NSubstitute. Entity methods take DateTime now as parameter for testability. Full compliance.

E1.description: Value objects (Plan, Money) are separate classes

A.notes: Plan is a sealed record value object. No Money class — price is decimal MonthlyPriceUsd on Plan.

B.notes: Plan and Money are separate classes extending ValueObject base. Money.Create returns Result<Money> with validation.
All properties: private set. Id is protected set from base class.

E3.description: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>

A.notes: Private constructor exists. Factory methods StartTrial/CreateActive return Subscription directly (no Result wrapping at factory level). Cannot express validation failures in factories.

B.notes: Private constructors. Subscription.Create returns Result<Subscription> with customerId validation. Money.Create returns Result<Money>.

E4.description: Result<T> pattern used for all operations that can fail

A.notes: All fallible operations return Result<Subscription>. Implicit operator for success path.

B.notes: Entity methods return plain Result (not Result<T>). Acceptable for void operations in DDD but technically not Result<T> for all.

E5.description: State transitions enforced inside the entity — cannot downgrade during trial

A.notes: Downgrade() checks State == Trial, returns failure. Enforced in entity.

B.notes: ChangePlan() checks Trial + IsDowngradeFrom, returns failure. Enforced in entity.

E6.description: Cancellation implements 14-day cooling-off with reactivation support

A.notes: Cancel sets CancelledAtUtc. Reactivate checks 14-day window. Both sides tested.

B.notes: Cancel sets CancellationDate and CancellationCoolingOffEndDate (explicit end-date field — superior design). Reactivate checks end-date. Both tested.

E7.description: Upgrades apply immediately, downgrades deferred to end of billing period

A.notes: Upgrade resets period immediately. Downgrade sets PendingDowngradePlan. ApplyPendingDowngrade at period end.

B.notes: ChangePlan branches: upgrade sets Plan immediately, downgrade sets PendingPlan. ApplyPendingPlanChange applies deferred. PlanChangedEvent carries IsImmediate.

E8.description: PastDue auto-cancel after 30 days is implemented

A.notes: ShouldAutoCancelPastDue predicate + ProcessTimeBasedTransitions in service triggers Cancel. Tested at day 29 and 30.

B.notes: AutoCancelDate field set to now+30 on MarkPastDue. Repository query GetPastDueForAutoCancellationAsync filters by this date. Service loops and cancels.

E9.description: Domain service orchestrates — does not contain business logic that belongs in entity

A.notes: Service is a thin pass-through. ChangePlan just routes to Upgrade/Downgrade. No if-chains in service for domain rules.

B.notes: Service delegates fully to entity methods. Only service-level concern is repository lookups and null checks.

E10.description: NSubstitute used for infrastructure mocks, real domain objects never mocked

A.notes: IDateTimeProvider mocked. Subscription/Plan created as real objects.

B.notes: ISubscriptionRepository and IDateTimeProvider mocked. Real Subscription/Plan objects used.

E11.description: Tests cover all state transition rules

A.notes: Trial→Active, Active→PastDue, PastDue→Active, PastDue→Cancelled(auto), Active→Cancelled, Cancelled→Active (cooling-off), upgrade, downgrade, trial-downgrade-blocked, boundary conditions all tested.

B.notes: Most transitions covered. Missing explicit PastDue boundary test (30 days) and upgrade clearing pending-downgrade test. Domain event assertions are a bonus.

E12.description: IDateTimeProvider or equivalent time abstraction used for testability

A.notes: IDateTimeProvider in Domain layer. Injected into service. Entity receives DateTime as parameter — both mechanisms are valid.

B.notes: IDateTimeProvider in Domain/Interfaces. Injected into service. Entity receives DateTime as parameter.

strengths: Extremely clean and focused — zero unnecessary infrastructure. Every file is directly relevant to the task.
Result<Subscription> on all entity operations enables fluent chaining and consistent error handling across the whole surface.
ProcessTimeBasedTransitions in the service elegantly chains trial-expiry, PastDue auto-cancel, and pending-downgrade in one method — easy to call from a scheduled job.
Boundary condition tests (14-day cooling-off at exactly 14 days, 29 vs 30 day PastDue) show thorough test thinking.
Full lifecycle integration tests prove the whole state machine end-to-end.
Plan as a sealed record naturally provides value equality without boilerplate.
IsUpgradeFrom / IsDowngradeFrom / IsInCoolingOffPeriod / ShouldAutoCancelPastDue / HasPendingDowngradeReady query helpers are expressive and readable.
weaknesses: No Money value object — price stored as decimal on Plan. Loses currency semantics, prevents future multi-currency support.
Factory methods (StartTrial, CreateActive) return Subscription directly rather than Result<Subscription> — no way to express validation failure at construction time without throwing.
No domain events — no integration-event story for downstream systems.
No ISubscriptionRepository abstraction — service tests cannot verify persistence interactions.

strengths: Full DDD infrastructure: ValueObject base class, Entity<TId>, AggregateRoot<TId>, IDomainEvent — proper layered architecture.
Separate Money value object with its own Create factory and validation — correct modeling of monetary concepts.
Subscription.Create returns Result<Subscription> with validation — perfect static factory pattern.
Domain events raised on all significant transitions (Created, Activated, Cancelled, Reactivated, PlanChanged) — observable and integration-friendly.
CancellationCoolingOffEndDate stored as an explicit field — DB queryable without runtime arithmetic.
AutoCancelDate field enables DB-level filtering (GetPastDueForAutoCancellationAsync) — scalable batch processing pattern.
ISubscriptionRepository defined — service tests can verify repo interactions via NSubstitute.
MoneyTests validate the Money value object in isolation.
PlanChangedEvent carries IsImmediate flag — immediate vs deferred observable in the event stream.
weaknesses: Entity methods return plain Result (not Result<T>) — less composable than Result<Subscription>; cannot chain or inspect the modified entity from the return value.
Plan static properties are computed (new Plan(...) each call) rather than singletons — equality relies on ValueObject structural comparison, which works, but means new object allocation per access.
ChangePlan consolidates upgrade and downgrade into one method, which is good, but moves the Upgrade/Downgrade distinction away from explicit named methods — slightly less expressive API.
SubscriptionTests use DateTime.UtcNow inline in some tests (not a fixed date) — minor non-determinism risk in edge cases.
Missing boundary-condition test at exactly 30 days for PastDue auto-cancel.

The 30-day auto-cancel check is split: entity has AutoCancelDate, but the Cancel call inside the service loop does not verify AutoCancelDate itself — it relies on the repository query pre-filtering correctly.

verdict.reasoning: Both outputs are high quality. Output B demonstrates deeper DDD infrastructure (ValueObject base, AggregateRoot, domain events, repository interface, Money value object) and a proper static factory returning Result<Subscription>. Output A scores higher on the specific rubric because: (1) it uses Result<Subscription> consistently on ALL operations enabling chaining, (2) test coverage is more complete with explicit boundary tests for all time-based rules, (3) the service ProcessTimeBasedTransitions method is an elegant solution to the time-based transition requirement, and (4) the implementation is remarkably focused and complete for the stated task without over-engineering. The deciding factors are test thoroughness and the Result<T> consistency throughout — which directly address the task's explicit requirements. Output B's advantage in DDD infrastructure (domain events, ValueObject base, Money, repository) adds production value but goes beyond what the task mandated, and its test coverage has small gaps. If the evaluation weighted production-readiness and DDD purity more heavily, B would win. On pure adherence to the stated requirements and test completeness, A edges ahead.

scores.note: Raw rubric scores are nearly tied (A:95, B:96 out of 100). Winner determination factors in expectation compliance — B is partial on E1 (A) and E3 (A), while A is partial on E1 and E3 (B). The tiebreaker is test completeness (E11): A meets fully, B meets partially. This gives A the practical edge on the stated requirements.

verdict.final_winner_reasoning: Per-expectation tally favors B slightly (3 vs 2 wins), but the two expectations A wins (E4: Result<T> consistency, E11: test completeness) are more central to the task requirements as written. The task explicitly asked for 'Result<T>' (not Result) and 'xUnit tests' covering all rules. A's test suite is materially more comprehensive. The overall implementation quality is very close — A wins narrowly on task-specific adherence.
